matches.x: had. heard that, they had anointed him king in the room of his father; for Hiram was ever a lover of Da-

matches.lb: Quoting Commentary: Rashi on I Chronicles 14:1:1
matches.x: And Hiram the king of Tyre sent It is explained in Genesis Rabbah (85:4): “This man was accustomed to being friendly to the tribe of Judah; so was Hirah the Adullamite to Judah.

[(Gen. 38:1, cont.:) < THAT JUDAH WENT AWAY FROM HIS BROTHERS AND TURNED ASIDE TO A CERTAIN ADULLAMITE > WHOSE NAME WAS HIRAH.] R. Judah bar Simon said: That Hirah from the days of Judah is Hiram from the days of Solomon;39Cf. Gen. R. 85:4. and throughout his days he loved this tribe, for so it says (in Gen. 38:12): HIS FRIEND HIRAH THE ADULLAMITE. And it says elsewhere (in I Kings 5:15 [1]): < THEN HIRAM KING OF TYRE SENT HIS SERVANTS UNTO SOLOMON > … FOR HIRAM HAD ALWAYS BEEN A FRI

6. Moreover Hiram, king of Tyre, when he had heard that Solomon
succeeded to his father's kingdom, was very glad of it, for he was a
friend of David's. So he sent ambassadors to him, and saluted him, and
congratulated him on the present happy state of his affairs. Upon which
Solomon sent him an epistle, the contents of which here follow:
had. heard that, they had anointed him king in the room of his father; for Hiram was ever a lover of Da-

succeeded to his father's kingdom, was very glad of it, for he was a

congratulated him on the present happy state of his affairs. Upon which
Solomon sent him an epistle, the contents of which here follow:
11 And Hiram king of Tyre sent messengers to David, and cedar-trees, and carpenters, and masons; and they built David a house.

11 And Hiram king of Tyre sent messengers to David, and cedar-trees, and carpenters, and masons; and they built David a house.

6. Moreover Hiram, king of Tyre, when he had heard that Solomon
succeeded to his father's kingdom, was very glad of it, for he was a
friend of David's. So he sent ambassadors to him, and saluted him, and
congratulated him on the present happy state of his affairs. Upon which
Solomon sent him an epistle, the contents of which here follow:

CHAPTER 5. How Solomon Built Himself A Royal Palace, Very Costly And
Splendid; And How He Solved The Riddles Which Were Sent Him By Hiram.
26 And king Solomon sent by the hand of Benaiah the son of Jehoiada ; and he fell upon him that he died.

CHAPTER 5. How Solomon Built Himself A Royal Palace, Very Costly And
Splendid; And How He Solved The Riddles Which Were Sent Him By Hiram.
Hiram blessing God for Solomon, furnisheth him with timber for the emple--The nun ber of Solomon's workmen. -

fir tree...cedar—if even the cedars (the highest in the state) are not spared, how much less the fir trees (the lowest)! (JFB Bible Commentary)
il (Now Hiram’ the king of Tyre had. furnishod Solomon with: cedar trees. and fir trees, and with gold, according to all hia desire,) that then
fir tree...cedar—if even the cedars (the highest in the state) are not spared, how much less the fir trees (the lowest)! (JFB Bible Commentary)
and palm trees, according to the proportion of every one, and additions round about.

And thus, as it is hasidah, there springs from it hesed (mercy), which is the primordial light referred to in the statement: “And God said, Let there be light” (Gen. 1, 3). It is thus that region of which it says: “fir-trees are her house”, where the word beroshim (fir-trees) may be read berashim (at the heads, or head), signifying that there is another world (Geburah) which has its habitation below and constitutes the Court of Justice of this world.

And thus, as it is hasidah, there springs from it hesed (mercy), which is the primordial light referred to in the statement: “And God said, Let there be light” (Gen. 1, 3). It is thus that region of which it says: “fir-trees are her house”, where the word beroshim (fir-trees) may be read berashim (at the heads, or head), signifying that there is another world (Geburah) which has its habitation below and constitutes the Court of Justice of this world.
